title: AV Blocks: First-Degree, Mobitz I, Mobitz II and Complete Heart Block

content.text: AV blocks after cardiac surgery

AV block means impaired conduction from atria to ventricles. It is particularly relevant after valve surgery, septal surgery, aortic root surgery, inferior MI, RCA graft problems, electrolyte disturbance and AV-nodal blocking drugs. The key bedside question is whether ventricular rate and AV synchrony are adequate for perfusion.

content.definition: PR interval greater than 200 ms with every atrial impulse conducted to the ventricles.
Progressive PR prolongation followed by a dropped QRS complex, usually AV nodal and often transient.
Intermittent non-conducted P waves with constant PR intervals in conducted beats, usually infranodal and higher risk.
No atrial impulses conduct to the ventricles; atria and ventricles beat independently with AV dissociation.

rows: First-degree
PR >200 ms, all P waves conducted.
May be benign but watch if new after valve/aortic surgery or PR >300 ms.
Mobitz I
PR lengthens then QRS drops.
May be vagal/drug/inferior ischaemia; assess perfusion and progression.
Mobitz II
Constant PR with sudden dropped QRS.
High-risk block; may progress to complete heart block; urgent escalation.
Third-degree
P waves and QRS independent.
Often requires pacing if slow or unstable; high concern post-valve/root surgery.

content.text: Escalate immediately for Mobitz II, high-grade AV block, complete heart block, bradycardia with hypotension, ventricular escape rhythm, syncope/altered consciousness, ischaemia, or loss of pacing capture.
Mechanism

The AV node, His bundle and bundle branches conduct atrial impulses to the ventricles. Surgical oedema, suture proximity, valve annular work, septal manipulation, ischaemia and electrolyte disturbance can impair conduction. Aortic valve replacement, mitral valve surgery, tricuspid surgery, septal procedures and TAVI are particularly associated with conduction disturbance because of the anatomical proximity of conduction tissue.

First-degree AV block delays conduction but preserves AV sequence. Marked PR prolongation can still reduce filling efficiency because atrial contraction may occur too early relative to ventricular systole. Mobitz I is typically nodal and may be transient, especially with vagal tone, inferior ischaemia or AV nodal drugs. Mobitz II is usually below the AV node and is dangerous because ventricular escape may be unreliable.

Complete heart block creates AV dissociation. The ventricular rate depends on the escape focus. A junctional escape may be 40–60/min and relatively narrow; a ventricular escape may be 20–40/min and broad. In a post-cardiac surgery patient with low stroke volume, either may be inadequate. AV synchrony loss also reduces cardiac output, especially in diastolic dysfunction, RV failure and valve patients.
Nursing decision-making

items: Identify P waves and ask whether every P wave conducts to a QRS.
Measure PR interval: normal 120–200 ms; first-degree block is >200 ms.
For dropped beats, decide whether PR progressively lengthens before the drop or remains constant.
Assess haemodynamic tolerance with arterial waveform, MAP, urine output, lactate, mentation, skin perfusion and vasoactive requirement.
Check pacing wires and pacing box availability in post-op patients. Confirm capture, sensing and thresholds if pacing is used.
Review reversible causes: potassium, magnesium, calcium, hypoxia, acidosis, hypothermia, ischaemia, beta-blockers, amiodarone, digoxin and calcium-channel blockers.

content.text: Clinical Pearls
Mobitz II and complete heart block are not rhythms to watch casually in CTICU. They can deteriorate to profound bradycardia or asystole.
After aortic valve or TAVI procedures, new bundle branch block plus PR prolongation deserves attention because conduction disease can progress.
A ventricular rate of 50/min may look acceptable on the ECG but still be too slow for a stunned ventricle.
Do not confuse non-conducted PACs with AV block. A hidden premature P wave can make a pause look like Mobitz II; get a longer rhythm strip and senior review.
AV synchrony matters. VVI pacing may restore rate but not atrial contribution; DDD pacing can markedly improve blood pressure when atrial and ventricular wires are functional.
Equipment Tie-In
Epicardial pacing

Temporary epicardial pacing is central to post-cardiac surgery conduction problems. Atrial wires allow atrial sensing/pacing; ventricular wires allow ventricular sensing/pacing. DDD pacing can preserve AV synchrony, but only if both atrial and ventricular wires capture and sense appropriately. Threshold checks should be performed by competent staff according to local practice.

rows: AAI
Sinus bradycardia with intact AV conduction.
Unsafe if AV block develops because ventricles may not follow atria.
VVI
Ventricular backup for bradycardia or AV block.
Loses AV synchrony and may reduce cardiac output.
DDD
AV block with functional A and V wires; preserves AV synchrony.
Requires correct sensing/capture in both chambers.
DOO/VOO/AOO
Asynchronous emergency or troubleshooting mode.
Risk of competition with intrinsic rhythm; use only when appropriate.

content.text: Monitoring

Print rhythm strips at slow speed or longer duration when diagnosing AV block. A single screen can hide P waves. Use calipers or ECG grid measurement: one small square at 25 mm/s is 40 ms; five small squares is 200 ms.
Targets and Numbers

rows: Normal PR interval
Longer than 200 ms is first-degree AV block.
Marked first-degree AV block
May cause AV dyssynchrony symptoms or low output in vulnerable patients.
Junctional escape rate
Often 40–60/min.
May or may not perfuse adequately.
Ventricular escape rate
Often 20–40/min.
High risk of hypotension and instability.
Temporary pacing output safety margin
Often set at 2–3 times capture threshold after testing, or per local protocol.
Prevents loss of capture as threshold changes post-op.
Sensitivity
Set based on sensing threshold and mode.
Too sensitive can oversense; not sensitive enough can undersense.
MAP
Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered.
Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output
≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours.
Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate
Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support.
After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium
Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk.
Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium
Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk.
Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: What defines first-degree AV block?
options: PR interval >200 ms with every P wave conducted
quiz.explanation: First-degree AV block is delayed AV conduction, not dropped conduction.

quiz.question: Which block is most concerning for sudden progression to complete heart block?
quiz.explanation: Mobitz II is usually infranodal and high risk.

quiz.question: Why may DDD pacing improve blood pressure more than VVI pacing in complete heart block after valve surgery?
options: It can restore AV synchrony when A and V wires function
quiz.explanation: DDD pacing can coordinate atrial and ventricular activity, improving filling and stroke volume.
